# Passage:
In Exemplar of Liberty, historian Donald Grinde, Jr. and communications scholar Bruce Johansen contend that “there is an abundance of inferential and direct evidence to support the thesis that American government was influenced by Native American political concepts.” Grinde and Johansen draw on both the oral and written traditions of the indigenous North American Iroquois peoples and the words of Anglo-American founders such as Thomas Jefferson, James Madison, Benjamin Franklin, and John Adams, to support their claim that “the League of the Iroquois, with its representative form of democracy, not only predated the United States Constitution but also shaped the evolution and development of the ideas that shaped that document.”  
Many Native Americans and educators have warmly received this argument, now dubbed the Iroquois influence thesis. In 1989, for instance, a New York State public school curriculum review panel recommended teaching that Native American traditions “such as the Iroquois system of governance have had an impact on the development of institutions and practices of the State of New York and the United States.”  
Many in the academic community, however, are not convinced by the influence thesis in general and Grinde and Johansen’s work in particular. Some scholars point out that Madison, in the Federalist Papers, surveyed models of government from Europe, as well as from ancient Rome and Greece. They also note that English colonists in North America were already familiar with confederations, having formed the New England Confederation in 1643, decades before they first made diplomatic contact with an Iroquois nation (the Mohawks) in 1677.  
Historian Philip Levy argues that parallels between the Iroquois League and the founding documents of the U.S. government are inexact: although Franklin’s title for the deliberative body (“Grand Council”) is the same as that generally applied to the Iroquois central council, Franklin’s proposed number of delegates was forty-eight, whereas that for the Iroquois council was fifty. Furthermore, writes Levy, Grinde and Johansen use only Richard Barry’s Mr. Rutledge of South Carolina to support claims about the transfer of elements of Iroquois political theory over a twenty-year period to the Constitutional Convention.  
According to historian Samuel Payne, Grinde and Johansen maintain that the Iroquois influenced the formation of the American Union in two ways. The first was through advice and ideas that Iroquois spokespeople conveyed directly to the authors of the Articles of Confederation and the Constitution. The second was through the Iroquois political practices that the Anglo Americans observed and used as models in devising a new system of government. In evaluating the latter argument, Payne says, it is important to distinguish among three images of the Iroquois: (a) the Iroquois as they actually were in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries; (b) the Iroquois as the authors of the Articles of Confederation and the framers of the Constitution understood them to be in the late eighteenth century; and (c) the Iroquois as they have been described by the proponents of the Iroquois influence thesis. In Payne’s view, only the image Anglo-American leaders had of the Iroquois could have influenced the formation of the union.  
Had Anglo-American leaders known more about the Iroquois League, Payne goes on to say, they would probably have concluded that it could not serve them as a model: “Iroquois society was fundamentally different from that of the Anglo Americans. The latter lived in a state-organized society, and all their confederation plans were designed for the government of a state-organized colony or a sovereign state. The Iroquois lived in a nonstate society.”
